content:
Here are all 10 deviations found on the specs defects page, listed in plain English:

1.  UPS N+1 vs N+2 conflict - The design basis report says the UPS should be N+1 (one spare module per group), but specification 26 33 53 part 2.1.2.A requires N+2 (two spare modules per group). These two documents disagree on how much redundancy the UPS needs.

2.  Switchgear standard conflict - Specification 26 05 00 says all medium voltage switchgear must be built to IEC international standards, but specification 26 13 26 says the same equipment must be UL 1558 listed (a North American standard). A vendor cannot comply with both unless they fabricate two different sets of gear.

3.  Day tank fuel capacity illegal under NBC - Specification 26 32 13 sizes the indoor day tank at 1040 litres. The National Building Code caps indoor fuel day tanks below 1000 litres. The spec is technically non-compliant with fire safety code.

4.  Battery runtime contradiction in same spec - Section 26 33 53 says the battery must support full load for 10 minutes in one place and 12 minutes in another place. A vendor can pass one test and fail the other.

5.  Unmeasurable "adequate" requirement in fire suppression (section 21 22 00) - The spec says piping shall have "adequate supports and expansion provisions" with no numbers, no spacing, no load rating. No inspector can prove it is wrong.

6.  Outdated fire suppression standard (section 21 22 00) - The spec calls out NFPA 2001 2008 Edition. The current edition is 2022. This means the design is based on a withdrawn standard.

7.  Unmeasurable "sufficient" requirement in switchgear (section 26 13 26) - The spec says stored energy for breaker operation shall be "sufficient for one close-open-close operation" with no minimum voltage, no energy rating. Not testable.

8.  Unmeasurable "adequate" clearance in switchgear (section 26 13 26) - The spec says "adequate clearances per manufacturer recommendations" with no actual distance. If the manufacturer changes their recommendation mid-project, the spec changes with no review.

9.  Unmeasurable "adequate" ventilation in generator room (section 26 32 13) - The spec says "adequate ventilation shall be provided to prevent heat buildup" with no airflow CFM, no temperature rise limit, no air change rate. Cannot be verified at commissioning.

10. Missing test conditions for CRAC unit COP (section 23 81 23) - The spec says the cooling unit COP must be minimum 5.2 but gives no entering water temperature and no airflow conditions. COP changes with those conditions, so the test is meaningless as written.

---

The most concerning deviation is number 1 - the UPS N+1 versus N+2 conflict. Here is why: the design basis report tells the engineering team to design for N+1, but the detailed specification that the vendor must build from says N+2. This is not a trivial paperwork mismatch. If the project follows the design basis, the UPS will be built with one redundant module and will fail the specification requirement for two. If it follows the specification, costs increase and the design basis is wrong. Either way, the UPS is the single most critical power path for the IT load, and this conflict means no one can confidently say how much redundancy this facility actually has until someone makes a binding decision. The fuel tank code violation (number 3) is a close second because it is a regulatory compliance issue, not just a coordination problem.
reasoning_content:
The user wants to know about all the deviations on the "specs defects page" - which is the "lint" page in this system. Let me also check the margins page and other pages that might have defects/deviations.

The lint page has 10 findings. Let me present them in plain English with no markdown symbols.
